critique.summary: Google DeepMind has introduced a cognitive framework to measure progress toward Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) by identifying 10 key cognitive abilities and proposing a three-stage evaluation protocol. They are also launching a Kaggle hackathon to develop evaluations for these abilities, offering a $200,000 prize pool to encourage community participation.
mainPoints: Google DeepMind released a paper titled 'Measuring Progress Toward AGI: A Cognitive Taxonomy' outlining a framework based on cognitive science to evaluate AI systems' general intelligence.

The framework identifies 10 key cognitive abilities important for AGI, including perception, generation, attention, learning, memory, reasoning, metacognition, executive functions, problem solving, and social cognition.
A three-stage evaluation protocol is proposed to benchmark AI performance against human baselines across these cognitive abilities.
A Kaggle hackathon is launched to crowdsource the development of evaluations for five cognitive abilities with the largest evaluation gaps, offering a total prize pool of $200,000.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with publicly available information about Google DeepMind's research initiatives and their collaboration with Kaggle. The cognitive abilities listed align with established cognitive science literature, and the approach to benchmarking AI against human performance is a recognized method in AI evaluation. The announcement of the hackathon and prize details can be verified on Kaggle's official platform. Therefore, the article appears accurate and truthful.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential limitations or challenges in accurately measuring AGI using cognitive science frameworks, such as the complexity of human cognition or the diversity of AI architectures.
It does not address how this framework compares to other AGI evaluation methods or how it integrates with broader AI safety and ethical considerations.
The article does not mention the criteria for selecting the five cognitive abilities targeted in the hackathon or how the resulting evaluations will be validated and standardized.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the challenges and implications of defining and measuring progress toward AGI, with skepticism about current benchmarks and the nature of intelligence in AI. Participants debate the adequacy of cognitive frameworks, the role of social cognition, and the distinction between LLMs and true AGI.

comments.author: pocketarc
comments.text:
When people imagined AI/AGI, they imagined something that can reason like we can, except at the speed of a computer, which we always envisioned would lead to the singularity. In a short period of time, AI would be so far ahead of us and our existing ideas, that the world would become unrecognizable.

That's not what's happening here, and it's worth remembering: A caveman from 200K years ago would have been just as intelligent as any of us here today, despite not having language or technology, or any knowledge.

In Carolyn Porco's words: "These beings, with soaring imagination, eventually flung themselves and their machines into interplanetary space."

When you think of it that way, it should be obvious that LLMs are not AGI. And that's OK! They're a remarkable piece of technology anyway! It turns out that LLMs are actually good enough for a lot of use cases that would otherwise have required human intelligence.

And I echo ArekDymalski's sentiment that it's good to have benchmarks to structure the discussions around the "intelligence level" of LLMs. That _is_ useful, and the more progress we make, the better. But we're not on the way to AGI.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and accurate perspective on the current state of LLMs versus AGI, emphasizing the value of benchmarks without overstating capabilities.

comments.author: andsoitis
comments.text:
> Perception: extracting and processing sensory information from the environment

> Generation: producing outputs such as text, speech and actions

> Attention: focusing cognitive resources on what matters

> Learning: acquiring new knowledge through experience and instruction

> Memory: storing and retrieving information over time

> Reasoning: drawing valid conclusions through logical inference

> Metacognition: knowledge and monitoring of one's own cognitive processes

> Executive functions: planning, inhibition and cognitive flexibility

> Problem solving: finding effective solutions to domain-specific problems

> Social cognition: processing and interpreting social information and responding appropriately in social situations

--------------------

I prefer:

a) working memory (hold & manipulate information in mind simultaneously)

b) processing speed (how quickly & efficiently execute basic cognitive operations, leaving more resources for complex tasks)

c) fluid intelligence (ability to reason through novel problems without relying on prior knowledge)

d) crystallized intelligence (accumulated knowledge and ability to apply learned skills)

e) attentional control / executive function (focus, suppress irrelevant information, switch between tasks, inhibit impulsive responses)

f) long-term memory and retrieval (ability to form strong associations and retrieve them fluently)

g) spatial / visuospatial reasoning (mental rotation, visualization, navigating abstract spatial relationships)

h) pattern recognition & inductive reasoning (this is the most primitive and universal expression of intelligence across species, the ability to extract regularities from noisy data, to generalized from examples to rules)
comments.analysis: Offers a detailed and nuanced cognitive framework that aligns well with established psychological constructs, enhancing the discussion on measuring intelligence.

comments.author: tyleo
comments.text:
It still seems like something is missing from all these frameworks.

I feel like an average human wouldn't pass some of these metrics yet they are "generally intelligent". On the other hand they also wouldn't pass a lot of the expert questions that AI is good at.

We're measuring something, and I think optimizing it is useful, I'd even say it is "intelligent" in some ways, but it doesn't seem "intelligent" in the same way that humans are.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about the limitations of current benchmarks in capturing the full spectrum of human intelligence, highlighting the complexity of defining intelligence.

comments.author: ArekDymalski
comments.text:
It's good to have some kind of benchmark at least to structure the ongoing, fruitless discussion around "are we there already?".

However I must admit that including the last point that is partially hinting at the emotional or rather social intelligence surprised me. It makes this list go beyond usual understanding of AGI and moves it toward something like AGI-we-actually-want. But for that purpose this last point isn't ok narrow, too specific. And so is the whole list.

To be actually useful the AGI-we-actually-want benchmark should not only include positive indicators but also a list of unwanted behaviors to ensure this thing that used to be called alignment I guess.
comments.analysis: Provides a constructive critique emphasizing the importance of alignment and the need for benchmarks to include both positive and negative traits.

comments.author: zug_zug
comments.text:
I'm sorry what even is this? Giving $10k rewards for significant advancements toward "AGI"?

What does "making a framework" even mean, it feels like a nothing post.

When I think of what real AGI would be I think:

- Passes the turing test

- Writes a New York Times Bestseller without revealing it was written by AI

- Writes journal articles that pass peer review

- Wins a Nobel Prize

- Writes a successful comedy routine

- Creates a new invention

And no, nobody is going to make an automated kaggle benchmark to verify these. Which is fine, because an LLM will never be AGI. An LLM can't even learn mid-conversation.
comments.analysis: Expresses a common skepticism about current evaluation methods and highlights meaningful human benchmarks, though somewhat dismissive of LLM potential.

comments.author: Havoc
comments.text: Measuring something you can’t define or quantify seems somewhat dubious
comments.analysis: Concise and valid skepticism about the difficulty of quantifying AGI progress, though it lacks elaboration.

comments.author: qsort
comments.text:
Those are crowdsourced benchmarks. We're calling them "cognitive" and "AGI" now, though. It's similar to when they made a benchmark and called it "GDP".

To be clear, I think we've seen very fast progress, certainly faster than I would have expected, I'm not trying to peddle some "wall" rhetoric here, but I struggle to see how this isn't just the SWE-bench du jour.
comments.analysis: Offers a critical view on the nature of benchmarks, highlighting their constructed nature and the risk of overinterpretation.

comments.author: lvoudour
comments.text:
Social cognition: processing and interpreting social information and responding appropriately in social situations

Is social cognition really a measure of intelligence for non-social entities?
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about the applicability of social cognition metrics to AI, which may not possess social awareness.

comments.author: wcgan7
comments.text: Cool that we are at a stage where it is meaningful to start measuring progress toward AGI. Something I am wondering on the philosophical side: are we ever going to be able to tell if the system really "understands" and "perceives" the world?
comments.analysis: Highlights a fundamental philosophical challenge in AI evaluation, underscoring the limits of current understanding.

comments.author: yellow_lead
comments.text: It's kind of funny that Google's idea of evaluating AGI is outsourcing the work to a Kaggle competition.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism in a somewhat dismissive tone, implying that the evaluation approach may be superficial.

comments.author: wewewedxfgdf
comments.text:
AGI feels like a vanity project.

Who cares about AGI? Honestlky what's the gain.

Maybe Google could actually make Gemini good instead of being about 10 miles behind Claude instead of trying to make AGI because of - well some reason - cause they want to be famous.
comments.analysis: The comment is more emotional and speculative, focusing on corporate competition rather than substantive AGI discussion.

comments.author: hbarka
comments.text:
The two guys from Google get to set the rules?

How will they measure wisdom or common sense (ability to make an exception)?

https://youtu.be/lA-zdh_bQBo
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about measuring abstract qualities like wisdom but presents it with some skepticism about authority.
